mnemonics.title: Depth vs Breadth Balance
mnemonics.content: Think of a swimming pool: BREADTH = surface area (how many lanes), DEPTH = how deep the water is (one lane going to the bottom). Can't have maximum of both without huge resources!

mnemonics.title: Horizontal vs Vertical Memory
mnemonics.content: H O R I Z O N T A L = HORI-ZON = like a horizon line - wide view across everything. V E R T I C A L = goes VERTICALLY down - deep into one area.

mnemonics.title: When to Use What
mnemonics.content: EARLY = Breadth (stakeholders, concepts, IA), LATE = Depth (usability, complex flows). Remember: WIDE early, DEEP late!

mnemonics.title: Fidelity Scale
mnemonics.content: LO-FI = PAPER (quick, sketchy), HI-FI = DIGITAL (detailed, interactive). Think: Paper = Low cost, Digital = High cost, both have their time!

acronyms.expansion: Low Fidelity - simple, quick prototypes using basic materials like paper or wireframes
acronyms.usage: Used in early design phases for rapid iteration and early feedback

acronyms.expansion: High Fidelity - detailed, polished prototypes with real images, fonts and working interactions
acronyms.usage: Used in later stages for stakeholder demos and detailed usability testing

acronyms.expansion: Information Architecture - the organization and structure of content in a system
acronyms.usage: Tested using high-breadth prototypes to validate navigation and content hierarchy

acronyms.expansion: User Experience - the overall experience of a user interacting with a product
acronyms.usage: Prototyping is essential for testing and improving UX throughout development

acronyms.expansion: Minimum Viable Product - the simplest version of a product that can be released
acronyms.usage: Prototyping helps define what features are essential for the MVP

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a building: HORIZONTAL = floor plan showing all rooms at one level; VERTICAL = cross-section showing one room with all floors/levels in detail
Use a grid: Breadth = number of cells covered, Depth = how many layers/levels within each cell. Can't fill entire grid deeply with limited time!
Draw a line from paper sketch (left) to final product (right). Place prototypes along this spectrum: paper wireframe → digital wireframe → clickable mockup → working prototype
Draw a timeline: EARLY (concept) → use WIDE/BREADTH; MID (iteration) → balanced; LATE (testing) → use DEEP. Like drilling - first survey wide area, then dig deep where it matters!

analogies.analogy: Like a test drive: a deep prototype is like test-driving a car extensively on different roads, weather conditions, and scenarios to understand every aspect of that one car
Like a car show: you see many cars (features) but only from the outside, can't test drive any - you get the overall picture but not detailed experience
Like a model home: one fully furnished room showing all details (cabinets, appliances, lighting) while other rooms are empty shells
Like an architectural floor plan: shows where all rooms are located but no furniture, fixtures, or details inside any room
Like architectural sketches before building a house - quick drawings to show ideas, easily changed, helps catch problems before expensive construction begins
Like storyboarding a movie: quick drawings on cards that can be rearranged, replaced, or thrown away - fast iteration before filming the real thing
